speaker: interviewer
text: It's 1995, and we are in Sydney, Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And camera's ready.

speaker: interviewee
text: John, we were talking again about Emalia Factory. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You wanted to say something about your impressions about Oskar Schindler.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my impression of Oskar Schindler is that some people have the impression that he-- that he did it-- that he had motives to make money. And otherwise, he wouldn't have done it, and that sort of thing. Assume-- assume, according to-- assume that is right, that he did it for money. But how many Oskar Schindlers were there during the war which saved 1,100 Jews? If there would be 50 Oskars like that, there would be 50,000 Jews saved. So no matter what was his purpose, the effect is that he saved 1,100 Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you would say--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the bottom line.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he jeopardized his own life in the process.

speaker: interviewee
text: He did-- he did jeopardize it, but not in such a way, because he was very clever to manipulate the German hierarchy. He had the money, he had everything. But the fact is that he did do it from the beginning to the end. Maybe from the beginning he had different-- different ideas. But right in the middle when he saw that the war goes the other way, he leaned more toward the Jews. All right. But how many-- as I still say-- how many did that?

speaker: interviewer
text: A very basic question. When he started the factory, do you think he cared anything about the Jews-- when he started the factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe, yes. I believe he did. The way he treated me, I believe he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: But would he have treated any other cheap labor the same way? That was his nature maybe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe that was his nature. His nature was to combine both-- to have cheap labor, to treat the Jews well, and to have benefit out of it. What's wrong with that? What's wrong with it? There's nothing wrong with that. How many people in the world they-- they-- they exploit the black people? Or the Americans, they exploit 200 years ago for the same reason-- much worse. So I think that he didn't do any harm.

speaker: interviewer
text: You've seen the film

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your impression of the film based on your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the is 90%, 95% correct. But the only thing which I'm disappointed in it, I-- I can't see Bankier in it. Bankier wasn't there. I mean, somebody who could play as Bankier. But he wasn't mentioned at all. Maybe for the reason is that he didn't want to be mentioned, because he died after that. He died, I think, quite a few years ago. Because he would be today in his-- he would be today 100 years old. He was at least 30 years older than me.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you're saying, based on your understanding of Bankier, and the important role you think he played, he should have been at least some sort of role in the film.

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct. I don't know why he was not mentioned at all. Must be some purpose in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any incidences in the film that you remember happening the way that the films says it happens?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember the incident where the man with the one arm-- I never saw this man. There was no such a man in Emalia. Otherwise, I would know it. There was no such a man. That is, I think they made it up to making him even better, but I haven't seen such an incident.

speaker: interviewer
text: But maybe there were too many-- more workers. You know, you start at 60, but the size of the workforce grew, didn't it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But you see, even if there is a thousand people-- like from my experience, even there is a thousand people on one place-- which were in Plaszow. And one was shot, everybody knew who it was, why he was shot, and everything. It went like wild between ourselves. Like wildfire. And the same thing-- if there would be such a man, there was not so many people over there. I would know it, or maybe one day I would bump into him. I have never seen a man with one arm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any rabbis being part of the workforce?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was not a rabbi, but there was one fellow who was very religious, because he laid tefillin every day in the barracks. You know, he was lucky to have that to him. His name was [? Kopita. ?] But I-- But there was no rabbi between-- nobody called him rabbi. He was an Orthodox Jew but shaved. He had a shaved beard and-- you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long-- you stayed in Emalia in the barracks till when? When did you leave the Emalia barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in the Emalia barracks until about 1940. Because the--

speaker: interviewee
text: '44. I'm sorry. '44. Because 1944-- January, I think. '44 we formed Brunnlitz camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you go directly from the Emalia barrack and the factory to Brunnlitz, or did you go--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. To the Plaszow first.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And how long were you in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very long.

speaker: interviewer
text: A couple of weeks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Something like that. We had to-- they made a list. And they sent the men first. But I don't know about the women, because I was not connected. But according to what I've heard, the women came later-- whoever had a woman. I was single.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Now what happened? They said that the men would go in one train, and the women would go in another train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did both trains leave at the same time?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I don't know either. I don't know. But the story, what was in the that I think could be true. That they sent them somewhere else, and the Schindler had to go and get them back. Get them from-- from Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, the film says the train went by mistake.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it could be by mistake.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or-- or Schindler was lied to.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or Schindler was lied to. Something like that. It could happen. And Schindler had a hard job to get it. Because they told him, why do you have these 600 Jews? Now we'll give you 1,200 others. Much better.

speaker: interviewer
text: But that's what the film says.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he insisted on these women, because he was [INAUDIBLE]. Still [INAUDIBLE]. And he bribed her for a lot of-- some of them were the wives, you know. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were back in Plaszow for the few weeks, any experiences in that period of time that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was still the same, with shooting and that other. But I-- I didn't see-- I particularly was not involved. I was in one barrack with all the-- most of the people from Schindler's list was there. And we were waiting. We were not even engaged in any kind of work.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now why were you sent to Plaszow from Emalia factory? Were they closing it down? Was there a decision to do something to the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Because we knew that-- that we had to go there. And from there, we had to go to Brunnlitz. We knew about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you knew before you left the Emalia barracks--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that you were only going to Plaszow for a short time.

speaker: interviewee
text: For a short time, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now can you tell me, the list-- what do you know about the list? How was it made?

speaker: interviewee
text: The list was-- the list was blown up as soon as it come hit Plaszow, because there was not 1,100 Jews in Emalia. Emalia couldn't-- wouldn't-- couldn't employ so many people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many did it employ at its peak?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually less than half. Not even that. Less than half. So all these people remained. Because when I came to Brunnlitz, I saw so many new people which I never saw it before. So the list, he blew up to 1,100-- he-- he did tell the Germans that he needed so many Jews. Because-- because when we came to Brunnlitz, we still had to-- had to put the machine in place. We didn't start manufacturing straightaway, because the machine just came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you took the same machinery from Emalia--

speaker: interviewee
text: from-- from-- To Brunnlitz. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell Brunnlitz? Sorry, I should've asked you before.

speaker: interviewee
text: B-R-N-L-I-T-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So you know when you arrived in Brunnlitz, that you saw a lot of new faces.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I'm asking you another question. How was that list created? What do you know-- how did you make sure you were on that list?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, I was from the first 60, which he insisted that were his skilled workers, number one. And number two, I make sure through Pemper. I went to Mietek Pemper. And I told him that I'm-- I am-- I have got no money and I've got-- I cannot offer him anything, but I want to be on the list. So he said, don't worry. You'll be on the list. That was all. I had to leave it to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: But now I'm asking another question. Why did you go to Mietek Pemper? What was his relationship to Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the one who had influence of making the list. All the-- because what he could have done it-- which-- which-- which-- which I was worried, and all the people from the real Emalia was worried-- that he will change me for somebody else for money. That was-- that was my concern. Somebody comes with big money, that sort of thing. So he said, I can do the same work as John Armer. And that's what I was-- I was afraid of this, too. And first of all, I was afraid that I didn't have money. And then I was afraid that I would be changed for somebody else more influential.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I'm asking-- Pemper was influential in making the list, yes or no?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But is this the Pemper that worked for Amon Goeth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, the film talks about this guy, Stern, making the list with Schindler. I'm asking you, do you remember the list being created the way the film tells it, or do you remember it a different way?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember that Pemper and all this-- all this of them. They are the ones who made the list. Not Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: And not Stern?

speaker: interviewee
text: And not Schindler and not Stern. I don't believe it. Because Schindler was only concerned-- only concerned-- Bankier and all the original people from Emalia. And the rest, he didn't worry. The rest, all right. He had-- he had a few people which he knew, but a lot of people which he didn't know it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now I'm not criticizing the way the list was made. That's not my position.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: My position is to understand from someone who was there another point of view of possibly how the list was created. I'm not saying you're right. I'm not saying you're wrong. But I'm asking, is your impression of the way the list was created the same way as what the film tells-- tells us in the film?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean by whom or how?

speaker: interviewer
text: The people involved in creating the list.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well to my knowledge, I never heard about Stern and not Schindler. I only knew that Pemper had very big influential. And the fact is, is that most of the people who were there, and who are still in the list, were living in Krakow. They are the ones who they didn't-- they didn't took any strange people. Maybe a few. But most of the people were the chosen people from Krakow, because Plaszow was formed by the Jews from Krakow. And they had all the Judenrat and all the kapos. They were from Krakow. So they knew each other. And they knew whom to put it in, whom not put it in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Pemper part of the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pemper was not Judenrat, but Pemper was his secretary. It could be, too. I don't know what role he had besides it. But he was his right-hand side.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Pemper, in his own way, was very influential.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very influential in Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Plaszow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: The film, if I remember correctly, shows Schindler going to Goeth, Amon Goeth. And in a-- in a roundabout way, trying to tell Goeth, I want more Jews. I want to have more Jews. And Goethe telling Schindler, what does it matter? What do you need them for? They're all going to the same place in the end.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's right. Maybe. That's how they say. But the point is, he didn't know that what will happen at the end. And he wasn't-- he didn't know whom to put on the list or not to put on. For him, all the Jews were the same. To Goeth, you're talking about.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So he must have left it to Pemper. To Pemper, to Schindler, and to Stern. I don't know. I was only concerned that I'll be on the list.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my main concern.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your way to do that was to contact Pemper?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pemper, yes. Because I knew that he had-- and the fact is that I was on the list. Maybe he had something to do with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was Bankier, Avram or Abraham Bankier on the list?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. He-- Pemper-- what Schindler would have insisted. Schindler-- Schindler had a few people which he wanted to have on the list, because there was a factory next door which-- with three people which he knew were working there. And he also made sure that they would be on the list. They were his connection with the factory, because they were manufacturing-- I think they were manufacturing cases, you know. To pack cases, you know. This fellow was still living-- these three people were still living with us in the camp, but they went to the factory outside. And they also were not doing physical work. They were running the factory. But they were not so important as Bankier, because Bankier was his main concern.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you looked on by other-- not you, but people working in Emalia. How were you, as workers of Schindler, looked on by other people in Plaszow, for example? Was there jealousy?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if it was jealousy, but they knew it was all right. And they were trying to get in, but they couldn't. I don't know whether it was jealousy. It was envy. Isn't envy jealousy?

speaker: interviewer
text: Envy, jealousy-- Similar.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it could be like that, yes. Could be.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there bad relationships--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bad feeling?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. It was not bad feeling. But when the list was made, they were scrambling to get on it. Everybody was scrambling to go on it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're saying people in Plaszow knew the list was being made?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. People knew it. People knew that Oskar Schindler needs 1,100 people for Brunnlitz. And they knew that it's a ticket to freedom. Because they knew from the main group of Schindler, like me and some others, that Schindler treated us well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now that I understand. But I'm trying to understand-- was the making of a list a quiet project of Schindler and Pemper--

speaker: interviewee
text: Could have been.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or was it publicly known that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not publicly. No. It was-- it was-- done under-- it was-- it was done in such a way that the certain people could go on it. That we were good. Would I not know Pemper, would I maybe be not so influential, probably maybe I wouldn't be on the list, either.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you say you were influential?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was influential for Pemper, you know what I mean? I influenced Pemper. Would I not him or would I not know-- Schindler had nothing to do with me to being on the list. He only saw me once or twice in the factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask, during your time in the ghetto of Wieliczka or Plaszow, did you ever get a tattoo or a number?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I got a tattoo.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll have a look at it after.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the tattoo-- I got it in Plaszow, I believe. I believe I got it in Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Were you given a number or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Just KL, which just means konzentrationslager-- concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So that was probably in-- in Plaszow, you think.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when? In what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Could be-- could be any time-- could be from the-- could-- could have been even on the time before I went back to the barracks, you know. I can't remember how-- when do I had it. But it was one day, they-- they-- they did put us together. And a doctor came, an old doctor, and he gave everybody KL. Took about a minute.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it could have been when you were in Plaszow around January, February '43.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the liquidation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before-- it could have been, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: The liquidation of the ghetto-- did you see it happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you hear it happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard it had happened, but I didn't see it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But could you physically hear when it was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't there. It was far away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who carried out-- Who carried out the liquidation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I believe that Amon did.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm asking you--

speaker: interviewee
text: That is-- that is the true story. But I wasn't there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. No, I understand that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't be here. Liquidation-- that was the reason-- that my opinion is if Oskar Schindler took his people from Emalia to Plaszow, because he couldn't leave them in the ghetto. Because if the liquidation come, the soldiers would not make any difference. Emalia not Emalia-- they'd take anybody. So to make sure that Bankier and all the rest of us are safe, he gave us to Plaszow. So we are in Plaszow. We're going from Plaszow to Krakow. This is my opinion.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I understand that. It's not the opinion or the feeling that I got when I saw the film.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Because I got the opinion-- and this is where I'm asking your opinion-- that he didn't know about the liquid-- he knew a liquidation was going to happen, but he didn't know when.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, he didn't have to know when. But he take precaution before that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: As soon as he heard it-- as soon as he heard it-- he-- he did something about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my opinion.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the liquidation was carried out by Goeth, as far as you're concerned, or as far as you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you arrived in Brunnlitz, you were there for about seven months, I think you said.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, approximately.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you arrived around late 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Late 1944. And we were liberated by May 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which guards were there at Brunnlitz? Were they German? Were they Lithuanian?

speaker: interviewee
text: German.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were German?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were not Lithuanian. German. Real SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were SS.

speaker: interviewee
text: SS. And the SS commander. But as I say to you again, Schindler was-- was very good with the commander. Therefore it was-- it wasn't so strict.

speaker: interviewer
text: Again your eating, your living conditions in Brunnlitz-- they were reasonable?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They were reasonable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Better than in the barracks of Emalia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not so good as the barracks, because we were more compound, but-- and not so good food. Food, we didn't have sufficient. But Schindler made sure that we have food because he went and bought on the black market for it. So we had enough-- we had enough to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Again, I'll ask you a question that was portrayed in the film. Do you remember celebrating-- not you personally, maybe-- but people in Brunnlitz being able to celebrate Shabbat with singing? No. I can't remember that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, that stands out in the film.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, but that was at the end of the film. No. Because you see-- maybe it happened, but I know one thing-- that I didn't wait.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- as soon as the war was finished and the-- the-- the Russian soldier came in-- in-- into the camp and he said, you are free men. I thought, all right. I took his word for it. So I did not stay five minutes in the camp. I didn't wait for anybody. I went straight to Brunnlitz city and I took a train to Poland. I don't know which way. I can't remember. That I went to Bratislava. And when I went on the train, all I did was show them that. And they didn't ask for money. Particularly in Czechoslovakia. They knew I was camp. So, I don't know what happened after. I went as soon as the camp was free. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did many people leave straight away?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. At least, I went on my own. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no. But you would have seen if other people walked out the gates as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably they went, too. I didn't have anybody there which I had to wait, so I went straight away.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I understand that. But I'm asking, did other people--

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't-- I couldn't-- I can't remember. But it wasn't like on the film, it was thousands of people waiting for [INAUDIBLE]. I think everybody went his own way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went to Poland. I went back to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, the film shows you-- everyone sitting at the gates, lying on the ground, waiting for this one Russian soldier to come and say, you're free.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Once-- once he come, we were free, finished. You didn't have to stay anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the night before? Everyone meeting Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't remember that either.

speaker: interviewer
text: Making a presentation of someone making--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. All I remember it-- that what I was told. I haven't been there. That Schindler, by coincidence-- I don't know what he did. He did with the commander. I think he had a quarrel or something, and he did push him, and he fall down the steps. That's what I have heard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who fell down the steps?

speaker: interviewee
text: The commander. That's what I-- but I can't remember-- but I can't remember. But that's what I was told just before I left, that Schindler did it, and then Schindler went away. But I wasn't there when they give him the ring. All these things, I haven't seen it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember him addressing all the workers the day before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. Everyone-- as we are waiting, I think Schindler was somewhere already preparing himself to go. Maybe he went already the night before. But I know one thing. That-- they made it on the film. That-- that as soon as this Russian soldier came-- finished. The camp was finished. Nobody wanted to stay in there for five minutes if possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: All I'm asking is corroboration of certain events. Whether they happened like this, or happened somewhat differently.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. With me it happened different.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: It can happen different. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, after you left Brunnlitz, you said you made your way back to Krakow.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Krakow, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Krakow, I stayed until-- I think, the whole 1945. The beginning of '46, I believe, I went to Germany to the Displaced Persons Camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which camp was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leipheim.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-E-I-P-H-A-M.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there-- it was the beginning of '46, right? I was there until about November, October '46. And from there I went to Sweden.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do during your time in Leipheim?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. We were just eating, drinking, and-- and waiting to have news. Some people went to America. Some people got permits to go to Brazil. I had a permit to go to New Zealand, so I went to Sweden first.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. But in Leipheim, did you go there on your own? With any other members of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: On my own.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went back to Krakow from Brunnlitz, did you find any other members of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I found my brother-- older brother. But my brother stayed in Krakow in Poland, because he was making good business over there. But I didn't want to stay in Krakow. So I said, I got a permit to go to New Zealand, and it expired in March 1948. So I only had a limited time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother was that you found?

speaker: interviewee
text: This one. The older brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Issak.

speaker: interviewee
text: Issak. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And how long did he stay in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- as a matter of fact, he stayed as long as I stayed in Leipheim. And when I came to Sweden, he came one day before me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this coincidence or planned?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Maybe it was coincidence, because they didn't know when I'm coming. But he-- he got a-- he went officially. He got a visa from Poland to go to Sweden. And I went through the Red Cross without a visa, without anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you arrived in Leipheim, you went from Krakow. Did you go via any other place to Leipheim? Did you go--

speaker: interviewee
text: I went-- yes. I went through-- first, I went to Bratislava, because we were smuggled there. We had to go through the mountain, the Polish mountain. Because in all of those days, the Polish border was closed. So we went through the-- through the mountain to Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were caught by the police of Slovakia. And we told them that we are Jews, so they sent us to Bratislava. And from Bratislava to Vienna, you know, to this hotel. What's his name? The Rothschild--

speaker: interviewee
text: Rothschild Hotel. And from there we went to Leipheim.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you went to Sweden. Where did you first-- where did you go first in Sweden?

speaker: interviewee
text: First in Sweden, I went to Norrkoping.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Can you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: N-O-R-R-K-I-P-I-N-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked in a factory. In a factory where they were making the material-- you know, a weaving factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And how long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was end of '46. I stayed until about January or February of '47. Then I went to Stockholm. And I worked in the factory where they were manufacturing-- well no, I was working for LM Ericsson. It's a very well-known firm. I worked outside Stockholm. Very good job. Very good pay. Not much work. I was working there. And from there I went to New Zealand in 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you make your way to New Zealand?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went by boat from Gothenburg to New York. I stayed in New York for about a month. I lived in the Jewish agency on the top-- I didn't go to a hotel because I didn't have money. So-- so I lived, you know, on top-- on top of the Jewish agency in the Lafayette Street. And then I went to San Francisco, across America by Greyhound bus. And I came-- and I came about the 3rd of March to New Zealand. And the end of March, the permit expired. [LAUGHS] Just before the permit expired, I came to New Zealand. And they knew about it, so they had to send me quick.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from San Francisco to New Zealand, I flew. I flew through Honolulu, Fiji-- because in those days, they didn't have jets. They had just-- Propeller.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had to go first to-- to Honolulu. Then we went to Kanton Island, then to-- to Fiji, and then from Fiji to Auckland, New Zealand.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you stayed in Auckland?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed with my uncle until 1951.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you came to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I came to Australia. And that is the whole story. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And in Australia, what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first-- the first year, I was working as a farrier for Cornelius. Have you heard about Cornelius? Yes. I was working there. And then I started a grocery business. And then I sold the grocery business, and I bought another grocery business. And I went into the photography business, but that's all in a short time. In photography, I stayed for nearly 30 years. And then about-- and it was 10, 15 years ago-- I went into real estate. And that's what I'm doing now. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. When did you get married?

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name of your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Trudy.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And what was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nadel. N-A-D-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in Sydney, are you a member of any synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Mizrachi Synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I believe you have some children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have three children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell me the names, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have-- the older daughter is Michelle. The middle daughter is Diana. And the youngest son is Ian.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Is Michelle married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Michelle-- you have to put that, too? You want to make the new generation? She was married, and she's divorced.

speaker: interviewer
text: But her current last name is Armer, isn't it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Armer. She never changed her name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And Diana?

speaker: interviewee
text: Diana is married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her surname now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is Heinrich.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: H-I-N-R-I-C-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And Ian is married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ian is not married.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: He is looking for somebody. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have grandchildren. How many grandchildren? Altogether, I've got five grandchildren. John, in concluding this interview-- let me first ask, is there anything you would like to add that we haven't touched on or maybe didn't go into in enough detail?

speaker: interviewee
text: All I want to say is that Schindler was a very good man. And I say it again, no matter what reason or what motive he had to do it, the fact is that he saved 1,100 Jews. That is very important. Therefore, we cannot-- we cannot run him down or say what he did it, because he-- because he did a very good thing. And that is the reason that he is now in Yad Vashem. He is-- he is buried in Jerusalem, and he has got a-- he has got a tree under the Righteous-- Righteous Gentile. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That is-- I have to give him-- I have to give him that tribute. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What else do you want me to say? On behalf of the Shoah Foundation, I'd like to thank you for your time today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a moment's time, I want to have a look at your copy of

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And meet your wife, and maybe some of your grandchildren, if they're here.

speaker: interviewee
text: You just missed them. They were just here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you want me to show--

speaker: interviewer
text: Camera is ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: John, could you introduce to us this attractive person sitting on your right?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Trudy. We've been married for 40 years. And she wants to say a few words. You say it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I met John at a dance at Bondi Beach Club. And-- and we got married on the 10th of June.

speaker: interviewer
text: In which year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have anything to say about John-- what he went through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know very much except what he told me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And you know he was part of the famous film now called

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, he was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. John, I believe you have a copy of-- or a photocopy of the original list--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which made up the Schindler's list. And if we can pan onto it, you can actually show us. I think you were number 595, is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on that list, you're down there as 595. And your name is listed as Jacob Armer--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was the name, the Jewish name that you were known by.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What profession did they have you listed as?

speaker: interviewee
text: Under the schlosser, which is metalworker. Metalworker

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you lift it up a little bit, and bring it forward? And schlosser is a metalworker?

speaker: interviewee
text: Metalworker.

speaker: interviewer
text: And like you, many people on that list had false professions put down there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: Again, on behalf of the Shoah Foundation, I'd like to thank both of you for your time this afternoon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Another interview, huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's the end of tape three of the interview of John Armer. It's the 29th of June, 1995. We're in Sydney, Australia. And that's also the end of the whole interview.
